docs.title: Lumina V3 Overview
docs.text:
Lumina V3, codenamed 'Chimera', is a cognitive autonomous trading system designed to move beyond linear algorithmic pipelines. Unlike traditional systems that separate data ingestion, feature engineering, and modelling into isolated states, Lumina V3 employs deep sensor fusion. It treats market data, news sentiment, and corporate structural relationships as a single, end-to-end differentiable computation graph README.md#14-32

The system is built around the "Thalamus" analogy: multiple sensory streams are merged into a holographic latent state before a central Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent makes a decision README.md#34-38

docs.title: The Chimera Architecture
docs.text: The architecture is divided into three functional layers: Perception, Fusion, and Cognition. This layered approach ensures that the agent acts on a unified representation of the market regime rather than raw, noisy signals.

docs.title: Key Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Dimensional Contract: To maintain the integrity of the fusion graph, vector dimensions are fixed. The system uses a 128-d price embedding, 64-d semantic embedding, and 32-d structural embedding, resulting in a 256-d latent state for the agent README.md#68-80
  2. Uncertainty Quantification: The Fusion Layer utilized MC-Dropout to estimate the variance (uncertainty) of its internal state. If uncertainty exceeds the UNCERTAINTY_THRESHOLD, the Uncertainty Gate prevents the agent from executing trades .env.example#77 README.md#51-56
  3. Adversarial Training (Spartan Curriculum): The agent is not just trained on historical data but is subjected to a three-phase curriculum including Behavioral Cloning, Domain Randomization (adversarial warps like "Flash Crashes"), and Sharpe Optimization README.md#243-249
  4. Hard-Rule Safety: A Safety Arbitrator acts as a final veto layer, enforcing non-negotiable risk rules (e.g., MAX_DRAWDOWN_LIMIT) before any order reaches the broker .env.example#78 README.md#59-62

docs.title: Cognition Layer: RL Agent and Training
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py
  • backend/cognition/training/curriculum.py
  • backend/cognition/training/trainer.py
  • backend/simulation/arena/schemas.py

The Cognition Layer (Layer 3) is the decision-making engine of the Lumina V3 architecture. It consumes the 256-dimensional latent state produced by the Cross-Modal Attention and DeepFusionNexus and outputs a 4-dimensional continuous action vector. This layer implements the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, integrated with an epistemic Uncertainty Gate to prevent execution during high-latent-volatility regimes.

The system is trained using the Spartan Curriculum, a multi-phase pipeline that transitions from supervised imitation to robust reinforcement learning under adversarial conditions.

docs.title: System Overview and Entity Mapping
docs.text: The Cognition Layer bridges the high-dimensional embedding space (from Perception and Fusion) to the discrete world of trade execution.

docs.title: Cognition Logic to Code Mapping

docs.title: Decision Flow: Latent State to Action
docs.text: The agent does not simply perform a forward pass. It uses Monte-Carlo (MC) Dropout to estimate the confidence of its own policy before committing to an action.

docs.title: Agent Decision Process
docs.text:
1. Input: Receives a 260-d vector (256-d Fusion latent + 4-d Portfolio state) backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#101-106
  2. Stochastic Sampling: Performs \(N\) MC-Dropout forward passes backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#186-192
  3. Uncertainty Evaluation: The UncertaintyGate compares the variance of these samples against \(\tau_{high}/\tau_{low}\) thresholds backend/cognition/agent/uncertainty_gate.py#1-10
  4. Veto Mechanism: If uncertainty is too high, the agent returns a defensive "Hold/Neutral" action backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#179-182
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Input Space"
        LS[("Latent State (256-d)")]
        PS[("Portfolio State (4-d)")]
    end
    subgraph "Cognition Layer (PPOAgent)"
        LS --> SC["State Concatenator"]
        PS --> SC
        SC --> PN["PolicyNetwork (Actor-Critic)"]
        PN --> MC["MC-Dropout Sampler (N=10)"]
        MC --> |"Action Variance"| UncertaintyGate["UncertaintyGate (Hysteresis)"]
    end
    subgraph "Execution Engine"
        UncertaintyGate --> |"Veto / Pass"| AV["Action Vector (4-d)"]
        AV --> ExecutionOrchestrator
    end

Sources: backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#167-182 backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#101-112

docs.title: PPO Agent and Uncertainty Gate
docs.text:
The PPOAgent implements the clipped surrogate objective to ensure stable policy updates. It utilizes a RolloutBuffer to store transitions including states, actions, log-probs, and value estimates backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#90-105

  • Action Space: A 4-D squashed-Gaussian space representing Direction, Urgency, Sizing, and Stop Distance backend/simulation/arena/schemas.py#94
  • Uncertainty Gate: A safety wrapper that monitors epistemic uncertainty. It uses a hysteresis mechanism to prevent "flickering" vetoes in volatile markets.
  • Policy Architecture: An Actor-Critic MLP that shares the initial feature extraction layers before branching into policy (\(\pi\)) and value (\(V\)) heads.

For a deep dive into the mathematical implementation and MC-Dropout logic, see PPO Agent and Uncertainty Gate.

docs.title: Spartan Curriculum: Training Pipeline
docs.text: The SpartanCurriculum is the master orchestrator for training the agent. It is designed to solve the "cold start" problem of RL in financial markets by using three distinct phases.

docs.title: Training Phases
docs.text:
1. Phase A: Behavioral Cloning (BC): The agent learns to imitate an "Oracle" (Kelly-fractional MA crossover) to establish a baseline of sensible trading behavior backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#12-14
  2. Phase B: Domain Randomization (DR): THe agent is trained on "Warped" episodes (adversarial scenarios like Flash Crashes or Volatility Spikes) to build robustness backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#15
  3. Phase C: Sharpe Optimization: The final fine-tuning phase where the reward function is strictly tied to the Sharpe Ratio and entropy is annealed to encourage exploitation backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#16-17

docs.title: Curriculum Pipeline Diagram
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Trainer
    participant SpartanCurriculum
    participant BehavioralCloningTrainer
    participant DomainRandomizationRunner
    participant SharpeOptimizer
    Trainer ->> SpartanCurriculum: run(bc_trainer | dr_runner | sharpe_optimizer)
    Note over SpartanCurriculum, BehavioralCloningTrainer: Phase A: Imitation
    SpartanCurriculum ->> BehavioralCloningTrainer: fit(policy | epochs)
    BehavioralCloningTrainer -->> SpartanCurriculum: bc_metrics (Accuracy Gate)
    Note over SpartanCurriculum, DomainRandomizationRunner: Phase B: Robustness
    SpartanCurriculum ->> DomainRandomizationRunner: run(agent | episodes)
    DomainRandomizationRunner -->> SpartanCurriculum: dr_metrics (Mean Reward Gate)
    Note over SpartanCurriculum, SharpeOptimizer: Phase C: Performance
    SpartanCurriculum ->> SharpeOptimizer: run(agent | episodes)
    SharpeOptimizer -->> SpartanCurriculum: sh_metrics (Sharpe Gate)
    SpartanCurriculum ->> Trainer: Save final.pt

For details on the adversarial generators and the acceptance gates for each phase, see Spartan Curriculum: Training Pipeline.

Sources: backend/cognition/training/curriculum.py#33-67 backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#1-31

docs.title: PPO Agent and Uncertainty Gate
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py
  • backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py
  • backend/config/constants.py
  • backend/simulation/arena/schemas.py
  • notebooks/06_uncertainty_calibration.ipynb

The Cognition Layer (Layer 3) of the Lumina V3 architecture is responsible for high-level decision-making. It consumes the 256-d latent state from the Deep Fusion Nexus and maps it to a 4-dimensional continuous action space. This layer implements a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent equipped with an Uncertainty Gate, a safety mechanism that monitors epistemic uncertainty via MC-Dropout to prevent the agent from acting in regimes where the model is "confused".

docs.title: PPO Agent Implementation
docs.text: The PPOAgent class manages the policy network, the rollout buffer, and the update logic. It follows the clipped surrogate objective approach to ensure stable policy updates backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#135-152

docs.title: Action Space
docs.text:
The agent operates in a 4-D squashed-Gaussian continuous action space backend/config/constants.py#26-33:

  • Action[0] (Direction): Range [-1, 1], representing full short to full long.
  • Action[1] (Urgency): Range [-1, 1], representing limit/maker to market/taker.
  • Action[2] (Sizing): Range [-1, 1], representing the fraction of risk capital (Kelly-fraction multiplier).
  • Action[3] (Stop Distance): Range [-1, 1], representing tight to wide stops in ATR multiples.

docs.title: Core Objectives and Loss
docs.text:
The agent optimizes a total function \(L\) composed of three terms backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#17-22:

  1. Clipped Surrogate Objective (\(L^{CLIP}\)): Limits the policy update step size to prevent catastrophic performance collapse backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#6-9
  2. Value Loss (\(L^{VF}\)): A clipped MSE loss between predicted values and GAE-calculated returns backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#21-22
  3. Entropy Bonus (\(H\)): Encourages exploration by penalizing overly deterministic policies backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#72-73

docs.title: Advantage Estimation
docs.text:
The agent utilizes Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE) to balance bias and variance in the advantage signal backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#11-16

  • \(\gamma\) (Discount Factor): Set to 0.99 for an effective horizon of ~100 steps backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#59-61
  • \(\lambda\) (GAE Lambda): Set to 0.95 to provide a smooth trade-off between TD(0) and Monte Carlo returns backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#62-64

docs.title: Training Flow and Early-Stop
docs.text:
During the update phase, the agent iterates over the RolloutBuffer for a fixed number of epochs (default 10) backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#76-77 It monitors KL divergence between the old and new policy; if the divergence exceeds \(1.5 \times \text{target_k}\) (default 0.2), the update is terminated early to prevent policy drift backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#82-84

Sources: backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#1-164 backend/config/constants.py#24-66

docs.title: Uncertainty Gate
docs.text: The UncertaintyGate is a "hysteresis-based" safety mechanism that sits outside the core RL loop. It evaluates the model's confidence in its own predictions using Monte-Carlo (MC) Dropout backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#27-31

docs.title: MC-Dropout Mechanism
docs.text:
When the agent's act() method is called, it performs \(N=10\) forward passes through the PolicyNetwork with dropout enabled backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py#186-191

  1. Sampling: \(N\) action vectors are sampled.
  2. Variance Calculation: The variance across these samples represents the epistemic uncertainty notebooks/06_uncertainty_calibration.ipynb
  3. Normalization: The variance is normalized to a \([0, 1]\) scalar notebooks/06_uncertainty_calibration.ipynb

docs.title: Gate Logic: Hysteresis and Veto
docs.text:
The gate uses two thresholds, \(\tau_{high}\) and \(\tau_{low}\), to manage state transitions:

  • Engagement: If uncertainty exceeds \(\tau_{high}\), the gate engages and replaces the agent's action with a Defensive Action (usually "Neutral/Cash").
  • Disengagement: The gate only disengages when uncertainty falls bellow \(\tau_{low}\). This prevents "flickering" in high-volatility regimes notebooks/06_uncertainty_calibration.ipynb
  • Critical Veto: If the gate vetoes 50 consecutive steps, it triggers a CRITICAL log entry, suggesting a potential data-feed failure or major distributional shift notebooks/06_uncertainty_calibration.ipynb

docs.title: Calibration (Threshold Sweep)
docs.text:
Thresholds are picked using a precision/recall trade-off. The goal is to maximize Adversarial Recall (catching 90% of adversarial regimes) while maintaining Coverage (allowing the agent to act in 80% of healthy regimes) notebooks/06_uncertainty_calibration.ipynb

Sources: backend/cognition/agent/ppo_agent.py notebooks/06_uncertainty_calibration.ipynb

docs.title: Behavioral Cloning (Phase A)
docs.text: Before PPO training (Phase B), the PolicyNetwork is warm-started using Behavioral Cloning (BC). This prevents the agent from starting with random noise and ensures it begins in a region of parameter space that produces coherent actions backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#2-8

docs.title: Loss Function
docs.text:
BC minimizes the Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) of expert actions under the current policy backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#12-16:

\[ L(\theta)=-\mathbf{E}_{(s,\alpha^*)\sim D}[\log\pi_\theta(a^*|s)] \]

This is preferred over MSE because it avoids tanh saturation at the action boundaries (\(\pm1\)) and effectively calibrates the variance (log-std) of the policy for future PPO exploration backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#20-29

docs.title: Validation Metric
docs.text:
The Spartan Curriculum uses a bc_min_accuracy gate (default 0.55). Accuracy is defined as the fraction of validation samples where the policy's deterministic action has the same sign-pattern (directionality) as the expert across all 4 dimensions backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#39-42

Sources: backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#1-43 backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#85-118

The Spartan Curriculum is the master training protocol for the Lumina V3 cognitive agent. It employs a three-phase progression designed to move the policy from initial random noise to robust, risk-adjusted trading performance. The pipeline transitions from supervised imitation to reinforcement learning under adversarial conditions, and finally to reward-shaping for financial objectives backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#9-17

docs.title: Pipeline Overview
docs.text: The training process is orchestrated by the SpartanCurriculum class, which enforces "acceptance gates" at the end of each phase. If a model fails to meet the required performance threshold (e.g., minimum accuracy or Sharpe ratio), the pipeline raises a RuntimeError and terminates to prevent the deployment of sub-optimal models backend/cognition/training/curriculum.py#34-67

docs.title: Phase A: Behavioral Cloning (BC)
docs.text: Phase A warm-starts the PolicyNetwork by imitating an "oracle" policy. This ensures that the agent begins Phase B within a region of parameter space that already produces coherent trajectories, rather than exploring from random weights backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#4-8

docs.title: Expert Demonstrations
docs.text:
The system supports three sources for expert data:

  1. Synthetic Oracle: A Kelly-fraction MA-crossover policy used for cold-starts backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#78-112
  2. Historical BC: Real latent states pulled from TimescaleDB and processed through the HistoricalEpisodeGenerator backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#146-158
  3. Feedback Dataset: An .npz file containing expert corrections or high-quality historical trajectories, typically loaded via scripts/train_from_feedback.py scripts/train_from_feedback.py#15-31

docs.title: Implementation Details
docs.text:
• Loss Function: Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) of the expert action under the SquashedGaussian distribution backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#12-29
  • Validation: A 90/10 chronological split is used to prevent temporal leakage backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#33-37
  • Accuracy Metric: The fraction of validation samples where the policy's sign-pattern matches the expert's 4-D action vector backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#39-42

Sources: backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#85-118 backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#136-160

docs.title: Phase B: Domain Randomization (DR)
docs.text: Phase B subjects the BC-warmed agent to the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) loop using a mix of clean and "warped" episodes. The goal is to build resilience against extreme market regimes backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#15-17

docs.title: Adversarial Warping
docs.text: The AdversarialGenerator wraps a standard episode to inject synthetic anomalies such as FLASH_CRASH, VOL_SPIKE, or SUSTAINED_CRASH backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#175-177 This forces the agent to learn the UncertaintyGate logic and defensive positioning when the latent state diverges from historical norms.

docs.title: Phase C: Sharpe Optimization
docs.text:
The final phase optimizes the policy for risk-adjusted returns. The reward function in LuminaTradingEnv is shaped by the Sharpe ratio, and the PPO entropy bonus is typically annealed to encourage convergence on a stable strategy backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#16-17

  • Objective: Maximize the sharpe_min_ratio (default threshold: -25.0 for the curriculum gate) backend/cognition/training/curriculum.py#29
  • Final Output: The best-performing model is saved as models/agent/final.pt and is accompanied by a manifest.json containing metadata like the git commit hash and training configuration backend/cognition/training/trainer.py#24-30

docs.title: Data Engine
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/data_engine/collectors/yfinance_collector.py
  • backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py
  • backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py
  • backend/fusion/state_assembler.py

The Data Engine is the foundational subsystem of Lumina V3, responsible for the end-to-end lifecycle of market data, news events, and structural relationship graphs. It bridges the gap between raw external data sources and the high-performance feature requirements of the Chimera architecture's perception layer.

The engine follows a tiered architecture:

  1. Ingestion Tier: Multi-source collectors (WebSocket and REST) that stream raw data into a Redis-based hot-cache.
  2. Storage Tier: A dual-backend strategy using TimescaleDB for high-fidelity historical persistence and Redis for sub-millisecond real-time access.
  3. Feature Tier: An abstraction layer that serves normalized tensors to the encoders and maintains the "Online Feature Store" for live inference.

docs.title: Market Data Collectors
docs.text:
The system utilizes a modular collector strategy to balance cost and data fidelity. While Polygon.io serves as the primary production source for 1-minute OHLCV and real-time news, the YFinanceCollector provides a zero-cost alternative for Phase 1 testing and deep historical backfills of daily bars.

  • YFinanceCollector: Implements a sparse-backfill strategy where daily bars are mapped to a single 1-minute record at 20:00 UTC to maintain schema compatibility with the ohlcv_1m hypertable backend/data_engine/collectors/yfinance_collector.py#187-202
  • Polygon.io: Handles high-frequency WebSocket streams for price ticks and news events.
  • Scrapers: Specialized collectors for social sentiment and supply-chain relationship extraction.

For details, see Market Data Collectors.

docs.title: Storage Backends: TimescaleDB and Redis
docs.text:
Lumina V3 employs a "Hot/Cold" storage split to satisfy both the low-latency requirements of the RL agent and the high-throughput requirements of model training.

Backend Role Key Entities / Tables TimescaleDB Historical Persistence ohlcv_1m, news_events, supply_chain_edges Redis Real-time State state:market:{ticker}, state:uncertainty:{ticker}, tick-latest

The TimescaleStore class manages an asynchronous connection pool via asyncpg backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py#83-96 providing optimized methods for batch inserts and time-bucketed aggregation queries.

For details, see Storage Backends: TimescaleDB and Redis

docs.title: Ingestion Pipeline and Feature Store
docs.text:
The IngestionPipeline acts as the system's "circulatory system", consuming real-time updates from Redis Pub/Sub, performing data cleaning (e.g., news deduplication via compute_content_hash), and flushing batched records to TimescaleDB backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py#19-32

The Feature Store abstracts the underlying databases, providing a unified FeatureStoreClient. It operates in two modes:

  1. Online Mode: Optimized for the StateAssembler to retrieve the latest embedding from Redis with sub-millisecond latency backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#309-312
  2. Offline Mode: Used during training to pull massive datasets from TimescaleDB hypertables.

docs.title: Ingestion Pipeline and Feature Store
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py
  • backend/config/constants.py
  • backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py

The Ingestion Pipeline and Feature Store form the data backbone of the Lumina V3 system. This subsystem is responsible for the transition from raw market events to structured, high-dimensional embeddings. It utilizes a dual-storage strategy —TimescaleDB for historical depth and Redis for sub-millisecond online retrieval— orchestrated by a batch-oriented ingestion pipeline with integrated backpressure and cleaning logic.

docs.title: Ingestion Pipeline
docs.text: The IngestionPipeline acts as the primary bridge between real-time data streams (Redis Pub/Sub) and persistent storage (TimescaleDB hypertables). It is designed to handle high-throughput market data while maintaining system stability through Prometheus-monitored metrics and backpressure mechanisms.

docs.title: Data Flow and Cleaning
docs.text:
The pipeline consumes data from two primary streams:

  1. Price Stream: Tick data subscribed via _consume_price_stream backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py#100-122
  2. News Stream: Global news events via _consume_news_stream backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py#124-158

Incoming data is buggered and periodically flushed to TimescaleDB. News data undergoes specific cleaning steps, including text normalization via clean_news_text and deduplication using content using content hashes backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py#19-23

docs.title: Backpressure and Metrics
docs.text: To prevent memory exhaustion during volatility spikes, the pipeline implements a backpressure threshold defined by INGESTION_BACKPRESSURE_FACTOR backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py#49-51 When buffers exceed this threshold, ingestion pauses, and a Prometheus alert is incremented backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py#105-112

docs.title: Feature Store Abstraction
docs.text: The Feature Store provides a unified interface for the Perception Layer (Encoders) to access features without needing to know the underlying storage implementation. It is split into Online and Offline components.

docs.title: Feature Definitions (FeatureDef)
docs.text: Every feature in the system is registered in a registry using FeatureDef. This defines the feature's name, its dimensionality (e.g., DIM_PRICE=128), and its TTL (Time-To-Live) backend/config/constants.py#56-66

docs.title: Online vs. Offline Storage
docs.text:
• OnlineFeatureStore: Optimized for sub-millisecond reads during live inference. It retrieves embeddings directly from Redis using keys prefixed by ticker and feature type.
  • OfflineFeatureStore: Used for training and backtesting. It queries TimescaleDB hypertables to reconstruct historical feature vectors.

Sources: backend/config/constants.py#54-87 backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py#31-32

docs.title: Embedding Ingestion and Serving
docs.text: The pipeline ensures that embeddings generated by the Perception Layer encoders are immediately available to the StateAssembler for the Fusion Layer.

docs.title: Data Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. Ingestion: IngestionPipeline moves raw data to TimescaleDB backend/data_engine/pipelines/ingestion.py#173-180
  2. Encoding: Encoders (TFT, FinBERT, GATv2) read windows of data (e.g., OHLCV_WINDW_MINUTES = 240) backend/config/constants.py#92-93
  3. Persistence: The resulting embeddings are written to the OnlineFeatureStore (Redis).
  4. Inference: The StateAssembler reads these embeddings to form the 256-d latent state for the RL agent backend/config/constants.py#81-87

It utilizes a multi-source strategy to balance cost and data fidelity, transitioning from free historical backfills in Phase 1 to high-frequency WebSocket streams for live operations.

docs.text: The collectors are designed as modular adapters that normalize heterogeneous data sources into the system's canonical formats (e.g., OHLCVRow for price data).

docs.title: YFinanceCollector (Phase-1 Strategy)
docs.text: The YFinanceCollector serves as the primary data source for the Phase-1 milestone. It provides zero-cost access to historical OHLCV data, allowing for the validation of the training pipeline before commiting to paid Polygon.io subscriptions backend/data_engine/collectors/yfinance_collector.py#5-13

docs.title: Implementation Details
docs.text:
The collector is a synchronous wrapper around the yfinance library. Since yfinance is not natively asynchronous, calls are offloaded to a thread executor when integrated into the system's event loop backend/data_engine/collectors/yfinance_collector.py#62-66

  • fetch_daily(ticker, start, end): Fetches daily bars. Used for long-term historical backfills where intraday precision is not required backend/data_engine/collectors/yfinance_collector.py#138-150
  • fetch_intraday(ticker, days_back, interval): Fetches 1-minute resolution data, limited by Yahoo Finance to the last 7 days backend/data_engine/collectors/yfinance_collector.py#154-175
  • _yf_to_polars(df): Normalizes the raw Pandas DataFrame from yfinance into a canonical Polars DataFrame with UTC-localized timestamps and standard column names (time, open, high, low, close, volume) backend/data_engine/collectors/yfinance_collector.py#69-134

docs.title: Sparse-Backfill Strategy
docs.text:
To maintain compatibility with the 1-minute resolution requirements of the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), the collector implements a "sparse-backfill" strategy. When backfilling historical daily data, it maps each daily bar to a single 1-minute bar at 20:00 UTC (NYSE close) withing the ohlcv_1m hypertable backend/data_engine/collectors/yfinance_collector.py#187-191

Sources: backend/data_engine/collectors/yfinance_collector.py#17-41

docs.text:
To avoid IP bans and API throttling, the collectors implement explicit rate limiting and defensive error handling:

Feature Implementation Purpose Backfill Throttle rate_limit_seconds=0.5 Prevents yfinance from flagging burst requests during multi-ticker backfills Schema Enforcement _yf_to_polars Ensures consisten types (e.g., pl.Datatime, pl.Float64) before insertion into TimescaleDB TZ Normalization dt.tz_convert("UTC") Standardizes all incoming timestamps to UTC to prevent alignment errors in the Feature Store Thread Safety asyncio.get_running_loop() Wraps synchronous I/O in run_in_executor to prevent blocking the main event loop

docs.title: Storage Backends: TimescaleDB and Redis
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • .dockerignore
  • alembic/versions/003_add_portfolio_and_backtest.py
  • backend/data_engine/storage/init.py
  • backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py
  • backend/fusion/state_assembler.py
  • tests/data_engine/storage/test_timescale.py

Lumina V3 utilizes a dual-storage strategy to balance the high-throughput, low-latency requirements of real-time trading with the complex, time-series analytical needs of historical backtesting and feature engineering.

The system partitions data between TimescaleDB (the "Cold Store" and Analytical Engine) and Redis (the "Hot Store" and IPC Backbone).

docs.title: 1. TimescaleDB: The Analytical Cold Store
docs.text: TimescaleDB acts as the primary persistence layer. It is used for storing high-resolution OHLCV data, unstructured news events, supply-chain relationship graphs and simulation results. The implementation leverages Hypertables to automatically partition data by time, ensuring performance remains stable as the dataset grows into the hundreds of millions of rows alembic/versions/003_add_portfolio_and_backtest.py#26

docs.title: Key Tables and Hypertables
docs.text: Table Name Type Purpose Key Columns ohlcv_1m Hypertable 1-minute price bars time, ticker, open, close, volume news_events Standard Semantic news data time, tickers, headline, content_hash supply_chain_edges Standard Corporate relationship graph source_ticker, target_ticker, weight portfolio_history Hypertable Equity/Cash tracking time, equity, cash backtest_runs Standard Metadata for backtest jobs run_id, status, sharpe

docs.title: TimescaleStore Gateway
docs.text:
The TimescaleStore class in backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py provides an asynchronous interface using asyncpg backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py#80-83 It manages a connection pool and provides high-level methods for batch insertion and window-based retrieval.

  • Batch Insertion: insert_ohlcv_batch handles bulk inserts with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING to prevent duplicates during overlapping backfills backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py#112-128
  • Time Bucketing: get_historical_window utilizes TimescaleDB's time_bucket function to aggregate 1m data into 5m, 1h, or 1d intervals on-the-fly.

Sources: backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py#1-313 alembic/versions/003_add_portfolio_and_backtest.py#17-42

docs.title: 2. Redis: The High-Speed Hot Store
docs.text: Redis serves as the low-latency state-sharing mechanism between the Perception, Fusion, and Cognition layers. It operates as an asynchronous cache for embeddings and a Pub/Sub message bus for real-time updates.

docs.title: Data Flow and Key Spaces
docs.text:
Redis handles four primary data types:

  1. Modality Embeddings: Vector outputs from the TFT (Temporal), FinBERT (Semantic), and GAT (Structural) encoders.
  2. Market State: The 256-d fused latent vector produced by the DeepFusionNexus.
  3. Deduplication: Content hashes for news events to prevent redundant procesing backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py#168-188
  4. Pub/Sub: Channels like channel:state.updates notify the RL Agent when a new fused state is ready backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#86

docs.title: State Assembler Integration
docs.text:
The StateAssembler is the primary consumer and producer of Redis-backed state data. It reads individual embeddings and writes the final market state to specific keys:

  • state:market:{ticker}: The 256-d fused vector backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#93-94
  • state:uncertainty:{ticker}: The MC-Dropout variance estimate backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#101-102
  • state:attention:{ticker}: Cross-modal attention weights backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#97-98

Sources: backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#1-142 backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py#59-68

docs.title: Fusion Layer: Deep Fusion Nexus
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py
  • backend/fusion/cross_attention.py
  • backend/fusion/nexus.py
  • backend/fusion/state_assembler.py
  • notebooks/05_fusion_parity_check.ipynb

The Fusion Layer (Layer 2) serves as the "Thalamus" of the Lumina V3 architecture. Its primary role is to ingest the high-dimensional embeddings produced by the three modality-specific encoders and synthesize them into a unified, 256-dimensional Latent State Representation. This fused state provides the Cognition Layer: RL Agent and Training with a coherent view of the market, resolving conflicts between price action, news sentiment, and structural supply-chain pressures.

The core of this layer is the DeppFusionNexus backend/fusion/nexus.py#37-48 which orchestrates cross-modal attention, gating, and latent projection.

docs.title: Architectural Overview
docs.text: The fusion process follows a strict "Super-Vector" concatenation strategy. Rather than projecting embeddings to a shared dimension immediately, the system preserves the information capacity of each modality (Price: 128-d, Semantic: 64-d, Structural: 32-d) by raw-concatenating them into a 224-d vector backend/fusion/cross_attention.py

docs.title: Data Flow: From Encoders to Latent State
docs.text: The following diagram illustrates how code entities withing the backend/fusion module transform modality-specific inputs into the final state consumed by the agent.

docs.title: Figure 1: Deep Fusion Nexus Transformation Pipeline
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Layer 1: Perception"
        TFT["TemporalFusionTransformer<br/>(128-d)"]
        DFE["DistilledFinancialEncoder<br/>(64-d)"]
        GE["GraphEncoder<br/>(32-d)"]
    end
    subgraph "Layer 2: Deep Fusion Nexus"
        RC["Raw Concatenation (224-d)"]

docs.title: 1. Cross-Modal Attention (DeepFusionNexus)
docs.text:
The CrossModalAttention block implements a multi-head self-attention mechanism where each modality is treated as a separate "token" backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#25-31 This allows the model to dynamically re-weight stream; for instance, prioritizing the Semantic (news) stream during an earnings call while down-weighting Price noise backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#28-30

  • Mechanism: Uses per-modality "read" and "write" projections to manage dimension mismatches inside the attention space backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#19-22
  • Gating: A sigmoid gate self.gate allows the model to up- or two down-weight specific regions of the super-vector backend/fusion/nexus.py#58-61
  • Uncertainty: The Nexus uses MC-Dropout to estimate epistemic uncertainty by running multiple stochastic forward passes backend/fusion/nexus.py#110-123

For details on the mathematical implementation and the training parity check, see Cross-Modal Attention and DeepFusionNexus.

docs.title: 2. State Assembler
docs.text:
The StateAssembler is the operational service that bridges the Feature Store and the Cognition Layer. It operates in two distinct modes:

  • Live Mode: Consumes embeddings from Redis at a 1-Hz cadence and publishes the 256-d state back to Redis backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#108-110
  • Arena Mode: Wires encoders (TemporalFusionTransformer, DistilledFinancialEncoder, GraphEncoder) directly to the Nexus for inline execution during simulations backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#146-170

The StateAssembler also captures RawAttributionTensors (byproducts of the forward pass like cross_modal_weights and vsn_weights_by_feature) which are used for Explainable AI (XAI) backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#40-66

For details on the service lifecycle and Redis integration, see State Assembler.

docs.title: Cross-Modal Attention and DeepFusionNexus
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/api/schemas.py
  • backend/fusion/cross_attention.py
  • backend/fusion/nexus.py
  • backend/fusion/parity_check.py
  • notebooks/05_fusion_parity_check.ipynb

The DeepFusionNexus (often referred to as the "Thamalus" of the Chimera architecture) is the second layer of the Lumina V3 system backend/fusion/nexus.py#2 It is responsible for integrating three disparate modality embeddings, Temporal (Price), Semantic (News), and Structural (Graph), into a unified 256-dimensional latent state that serves as the observation vector for the RL Agent backend/fusion/nexus.py#6-13

docs.title: Architectural Overview
docs.text: The Nexus operates on a "Super-Vector" approach. Instead of projecting all modalities to a shared dimension first, the raw embeddings are concatenated to preserve their specific information capacities before being processed by a Cross-Modal Attention block backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#6-11

docs.title: Data Flow and Dimensionality
docs.text:
The fusion process follows a strict dimensional contract:

  1. Input: Price (128-d) + Semantic (64-d) + Graph (32-d) = 224-d Super-Vector backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#11
  2. Attention: Multi-head self-attention re-weights the streams backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#13
  3. Gating: A learned sigmoid mask scales the re-weighted vector backend/fusion/nexus.py#58-61
  4. Projection: A deepened MLP projects the 224-d vector to the final 256-d Latent State backend/fusion/nexus.py#63-73

docs.title: DeepFusionNexus System Context
docs.text: The following diagram illustrates how the code entities within the backend.fusion module bridge the gap between raw encoder outputs and the RL policy input.

    MC --> |"256-d Latent State"| PPO

Sources: backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#49-118 backend/fusion/nexus.py#37-74

docs.title: Cross-Modal Attention (Tsai et al. 2019)
docs.text: The CrossModalAttention block implements a variation of the Multimodal Transformer backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#35-36 Because the input dimensions differ (128, 64, 32), the block uses interanl "read" and "write" projections to allow a standard Transformer encoder to operate on the streams as a sequence of three tokens backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#19-23

docs.title: Implementation Details
docs.text:
• Read Projections: Linear layers (read_price, read_semantic, read_graph) project each modality to a shared d_attn space (default 512) backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#83-86
  • Modality Embeddings: A learned parameter modality_embed is added to the tokens to identify the source stream (Price vs. News vs. Graph) backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#112
  • Write Projections: After passing through nn.TransformerEncoderLayer backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#96 tokens are projected back to their native dimensions backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#89-91
  • Residual Connection: The re-weighted output is added to the original input embeddings (native-space residual) backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#170-172

Sources: backend/fusion/cross_attention.py#49-118

docs.title: Sigmoid Gating Mechanism
docs.text:
After attention, the Nexus applies a learned soft mask. The gate is a linear layer followed by a Sigmoid activation backend/fusion/nexus.py#58-61 It produces values in \((0,1)\) that are used for residual gating:

\[ \text{output}=\text{fused}\times (1.0+\text{gate}) \]

This ensures that the model can up-weight critical regions of the super-vector without the risk of the signal vanishing. backend/fusion/nexus.py#98-99

docs.title: MC-Dropout Uncertainty
docs.text:
The Nexus provides a method encode_with_uncertainty to estimate epistemic uncertainty backend/fusion/nexus.py#110

  1. It triggers \(N\) stochastic forward passes (default MC_DROPOUT_SAMPLES) with dropout enabled backend/fusion/nexus.py#126-131
  2. It calculates the mean and std across these samples backend/fusion/nexus.py#133
  3. The std represents the fusion-level uncertainty, which the StateAssembler uses to trigger the agent's Uncertainty Gate backend/fusion/nexus.py#18-21

Sources: backend/fusion/nexus.py#110-133

docs.title: Nexus Regime Warmup
docs.text: The Nexus weights are given a supervised "warmup" via the DailyNexusDemoModel wrapper backend/simulation/article_simulation.py#180-192 which attaches an auxiliary regime_head to train the Nexus to classify market regimes (Crash, Bear, Flat, Bull) based on the signatures in the super-vector backend/simulation/article_simulation.py#61 Driven by train_daily_nexus backend/simulation/article_simulation.py#521 this ensures the RL agent receives a stable representation from the start of its training.

docs.title: Parity Check (V2 vs V3)
docs.text:
A parity check is conducted to ensure the Chimera (V3) architecture outperforms the simple MLP/LSTM baseline (V2) notebooks/05_fusion_parity_check.ipynb

  • Criterion: V3 Sharpe Ratio \(\ge\) V2 Sharpe Ratio on held-out data notebooks/05_fusion_parity_check.ipynb
  • Regime Signatures: Synthetic data is generated where specific regimes have unique biases in one modality (e.g., "Crash" is dominated by the Graph signal) notebooks/05_fusion_parity_check.ipynb
  • Results: The Nexus converges over 50 epochs, achieving lower MSE loss and higher regime classification accuracy than the baseline notebooks/05_fusion_parity_check.ipynb

Sources: notebooks/05_fusion_parity_check.ipynb backend/fusion/parity_check.py#99-185

docs.title: State Assembler
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py
  • backend/fusion/state_assembler.py
  • tests/fusion/test_state_assembler_modes.py

The StateAssembler is the primary driver of the Lumina V3 Fusion Layer. It serves as the bridge between the three modality-specific encoder (Perception Layer) and the Reinforcement Learning agent (Cognition Layer). Its main responsibility is to aggregate embeddings from the Feature Store, pass them through the DeepFusionNexus to generate a unified 256-d latent state, and manage uncertainty estimation via MC-Dropout backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#1-14

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text:
The service operates in two distinct modes to ensure that simulation overhead (like XAI attribution capturing) never impacts live trading performance:

  1. Live Mode (arena_mode=False): A 1-Hz daemon that consumes embeddings from Redis, computes the fused state, and publishes it back to Redis backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#108-110
  2. Arena Mode (arena_mode=True): An inline execution path used by the Spartan Arena to run the full end-to-end pipeline (Encoders + Nexus) in a single process for high-throughput simulation and XAI data collection backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#105-111

docs.title: Data Flow: Live Reflex Arc
docs.text:
In live operation, the StateAssembler follows a periodic "Tick-Assemble-Publish" loop:

Step Action Description 1 Poll Every interval_s (default 1.0s), the service checks for new embeddings backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#299-301 2 Fetch Retrieves price (128-d), news (64-d), and graph (32-d) embeddings from the FeatureStoreClient backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#309-312 3 Fuse Calls DeepFusionNexus froward pass to produce the 256-d latent space backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#335-338 4 Sample Periodically performs multiple forward passes with MC-Dropout to estimate state uncertainty backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#348-356 5 Persist Writes the state, attention weights, and uncertainty to Redis and notifies subscribers via channel:state.updates backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#358-369

Sources: backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#289-369 backend/fusion/nexus.py#1-20

In live operation the three Perception encoders run as independent services that publish their embeddings to RedisCache. The StateAssembler reads them back through FeatureStoreClient.get_bundle(), fuses them with DeepFusionNexus, writes the resulting state:* keys to Redis, and announces the update on channel:state.updates. The LuminaTradingEnv consumes that state as the observation for the PPOAgent.

Sources: backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#86-148 backend/data_engine/storage/redis_cache.py#51-75 backend/simulation/environments/base_env.py#82-132

docs.title: State Assembly Loop
docs.text: The run() method implements the live 1-Hz loop. It utilizes the FeatureStoreClient in online mode to minimize latency. If any modality embedding is missing for a specific ticker, the bundle is marked as incomplete and skipped to prevent the agent from acting on stale or partial data backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#289-330

docs.title: Uncertainty Estimation
docs.text: Uncertainty is calculated using Monte Carlo Dropout. Every \(N\) ticks (defined by compute_uncertainty_every_n), the assembler performs uncertainty_samples forward passes through the Nexus with dropout enabled. The variance of these samples is stores as the uncertainty estimate, which the UncertaintyGate in the Cognition layer uses to potentially veto trades backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#348-356

docs.title: Arena Mode and XAI Attribution
docs.text:
In simulation, the StateAssembler uses the build() method. This allows the Spartan Arena to pass raw tensors (price windows, news tokens) directly. The assembler then coordinates the internal encoders and returns a RawAttributionTensors object backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#42-69

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RawAttributionTensors:
    cross_modal_weights: torch.Tensor       # (3,) softmaxed weights
    vsn_weights_by_feature: dict[str, torch.Tensor] # TFT VSN importance
    gat_edge_index: torch.Tensor            # Graph topology
    gat_alpha: torch.Tensor                 # GAT attention coefficients
    ticker_list: tuple[str, ...]            # Symbol mapping

Sources: backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#42-69 backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#178-189

Attention state:attention:{ticker} Softmax weights for [Price, News, Graph]. Uncertainty state:uncertainty:{ticker} Scalar float representing MC-Dropout variance. Updates channel:state.updates Pub/Sub channel for notifying the LuminaTradingEnv.

This page provides a comprehensive guide for initializing the Lumina V3 "Chimera" development environment. It covers dependency management with uv, configuration via Pydantic settings, and the containerized orchestration of the multi-service architecture.

docs.title: 1. Environment Prerequisites
docs.text:
Lumina V3 requires a Linux or macOS environment with the following specifications:

  • Python 3.11: Explicitly pinned in .python-version#1 and pyproject.toml#27
  • uv: The project uses uv for ultra-fast dependency resolution and virtual environment management Makefile#4-5
  • Docker & Docker Compose: Required for running the full stack, including TimescaleDB and Redis.
  • NVIDIA GPU (Optional): Recommended for the Perception and Cognition layers. The stack supports CUDA 12.4 (standard) and CUDA 12.8 (Blackwell) Makefile#35-36

docs.title: 2. Local Installation
docs.text: The project utilizes a layered dependency strategy defined in pyproject.toml#6-15 This allows individual services (API, Data, Brain) to install only the necessary sub-packages.

docs.title: Step-by-Step Setup
docs.text:
1. Clone and Sync: Use the Makefile to synchronize the environment with all optional extras (dev, api, data, perception, brain, gpu).

    make install

    Source: Makefile#52-53

  2. Configuration: Copy the template environment file:

    cp .env.example .env

    Source: .env.example#1-5

  3. Database Migrations: Apply Alembic migrations to initialize the TimescaleDB schema (hypertables, news events, and portfolio tables).

    make migrate

    Source: Makefile#86-89

docs.title: 3. Configuration & Environment Variables
docs.text: Lumina V3 uses a centralized configuration system powered by pydantic-settings. The Settings class in backend/config/settings.py#43-158 acts a singleton that validates environment variables at runtime.

The Lumina V3 "Chimera" architecture is a deep-fusion trading system designed to move beyond linear pipelines. Instead of isolated stages, it implements a holographic representation where market data modalities (temporal, semantic, and structural) are fused into a single latent state before being processed by a cognitive RL agent. This page details the end-to-end flow from raw ingestion to broker execution.

docs.title: 1. The Chimera Three-Layer Architecture
docs.text:
The system is structured into three distinct layers, mimicking the biological thalamus for sensory integration.

  1. Perception Layer (Encoders): Modality-specific neural networks transform raw, heterogeneous data into fixed-dimensional embeddings.
  2. Fusion Layer (Deep Fusion Nexus): A cross-modal attention mechanism that merges embeddings into a unified 256-d "Latent State".
  3. Cognition Layer (RL Agent): A PPO-based agent that reasons on the latent state to produce a 4-D continuous action vector.

    subgraph "Execution & Safety"
        direction TB
        PN --> |"4-D action"| SAf["SafetyArbitrator"]
        SAf --> EO["ExecutionOrchestrator"]
    end

Sources: README.md#40-66 backend/config/constants.py#10-22 backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#98-111

docs.title: 2. Dimensional Contract and Latency Budget
docs.text:
To ensure end-to-end differentiability and prevent dimension-mismatch bugs, Lumina V3 enforces a strict Dimensional Contract.

Component Symbol Value Code Definition Price Embedding DIM_PRICE 128 backend/config/constants.py#56-57 Semantic Embedding DIM_SEMANTIC 64 backend/config/constants.py#59-60 Structural Embedding DIM_GRAPH 32 backend/config/constants.py#62-63 Fused Super-Vector DIM_FUSED 224 backend/config/constants.py#65-66 Agent Latent State NEXUS_OUTPUT_DIM 256 backend/config/constants.py#81-82 Action Space ACTION_DIM 4 backend/config/constants.py#71-78

docs.title: Latency Budget
docs.text: The system is designed for sub-second reflex arcs. The DistilledFinancialEncoder is specifically optimized to meet a < 100 ms inference budget on a single CPU core README.md#138-139 The StateAssembler monitors this via Prometheus histograms with buckets ranging from 5ms to 500ms backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#75-79

docs.title: 3. End-to-End Data Flow
docs.text: The data flow operates in two distinct modes: Live Execution (decoupled via Redis) and Arena Mode (inline for attribution).

docs.title: 3.1. Live Execution Flow
docs.text:
In live mode, the StateAssembler acts as a high-frequency driver (default 1-Hz cadence) backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#126

  1. Ingestion: MarketDataCollectors push raw data to TimescaleStore (cold) and RedisCache (hot) backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py#80-128
  2. Encoding: Modality-specific services read raw data, produce embeddings, and write them back to Redis via the FeatureStoreClient.
  3. Assembly: The StateAssembler pulls the three latest embeddings from Redis, concatenates them into a 224-d super-vector, and passes them to the DeepFusionNexus backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#326-337
  4. Fusion: The Nexus applies CrossModalAttention and a sigmoid gating mechanism to produce the 256-d latent state backend/fusion/nexus.py#84-104
  5. Uncertainty: Epistemic uncertainty is calculated via MC-Dropout (stochastic forward passes) backend/fusion/nexus.py#110-133

docs.title: Data Flow: From Raw Ingestion to Latent State
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant TimescaleDB
    participant RedisCache
    participant Encoders as "Encoders (TFT/LLM/GAT)"
    participant StateAssembler
    participant DeepFusionNexus
    Note over TimescaleDB, RedisCache: "Raw Data Ingestion"
    Encoders ->> RedisCache: "GET raw_ticks / news_text"
    Encoders ->> Encoders: "Inference (Forward Pass)"
    Encoders ->> RedisCache: "SET embedding:ticker:type"
    loop "Every 1.0s"
        StateAssembler ->> RedisCache: "MGET [price | semantic | graph] embeddings"
        StateAssembler ->> DeepFusionNexus: "forward(price | sem | graph)"
        DeepFusionNexus ->> StateAssembler: "return {market_state, gate_values}"
        StateAssembler ->> RedisCache: "SET state:market:ticker"
        StateAssembler ->> RedisCache: "PUBLISH channel:state.updates"
    end

Sources: backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#306-369 backend/fusion/nexus.py#110-133 backend/data_engine/storage/timescale.py#112-128

docs.title: 3.2 Cognition and Execution Flow
docs.text:
The PPOAgent consumes the market state to produce trade actions.

  1. Policy Evaluation: The PolicyNetwork processes the 256-d state to output a Squashed Gaussian distribution over the 4-D action space backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py#20-25
  2. Safety Check: The action is intercepted by the SafetyArbitrator, which applies hard-coded rules (e.g., max drawdown, position limits) README.md#59-62
  3. Execution: If approved, the ExecutionOrchestrator maps the continuous vector [direction, urgency, sizing, stop_distance] to specific broker orders (Alpaca/Paper) backend/config/constants.py#71-78

docs.title: 4. Feature Store and State Assembly
docs.text: The FeatureStoreClient provides the abstraction layer for both Online (Redis) and Offline (TimescaleDB) access.

docs.title: The StateAssembler Modes
docs.text:
The StateAssembler class backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#105 is the critical junction between Perception and Cognition. It supports two operational modes:

  • Standard Mode (arena_mode=False): Used in production. It consumes embeddings asynchronously from Redis to minimize coupling between encoders and the agent backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#108-110
  • Arena Mode (arena_mode=True): Used during training and "Spartan Arena" simulations. It runs the entire pipeline (Encoders + Nexus) inline via the build() method to capture RawAttributionTensors for XAI backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#171-182

Raw Attribution Tensors captured during Arena mode includes:

  • cross_modal_weights: Softmaxed weights showing importance of Price vs News vs Graph backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#53-54
  • vsn_weights_by_feature: TFT Variable Selection Network weights showing which OHLCV indicators were active backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#55-56
  • gat_alpha: Attention coefficients from the Graph Encoder backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#59-60

Sources: backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#39-68 backend/fusion/state_assembler.py#140-170 backend/config/constants.py#92-102

docs.title: Perception Layer (Encoders)
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/perception/semantic/inference.py
  • backend/perception/structural/inference.py
  • backend/perception/temporal/inference.py

The Perception Layer constitutes Layer 1 of the Chimera architecture. Its primary objective is to transform raw, multi-modal market data into high-dimensional latent embeddings. These embeddings represent three distinct facets of market reality: price dynamics (Temporal), market sentiment (Semantic), and relational dependencies (Structural).

Each encoder operates as an independent microservice, consuming data from the Data Engine and publishing embeddings to the RedisCache via the EmbeddingWriter backend/perception/common/embedding_writer.py These embeddings are later consumed by the Fusion Layer: Deep Fusion Nexus to form a unified market state.

docs.title: High-Level Architecture
docs.text: The Perception Layer bridges the gap between raw data streams (OHLCV, News, Supply-Chain) and the "Code Entity Space" of the Chimera system.

docs.title: Data Flow to Latent Space
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Raw Data Space"
        ohlcv["TimescaleDB (`ohlcv_1m`)"]
        Redis["Redis (`channel:news:global`)"]
        SupplyChain["TimescaleDB (`supply_chain_edges`)"]
    end

    subgraph "Perception Layer (Encoders)"
        ohlcv --> TFTInferenceService
        Redis --> SemanticInferenceService
        SupplyChain --> GraphInferenceService
    end

    subgraph "Embedding Space (Redis)"
        TFTInferenceService --> price["'price' embedding (128-d)"]
        SemanticInferenceService --> semantic["'semantic' embedding <br/> (64-d)"]
        GraphInferenceService --> Graph["'graph' embedding (32-d)"]
    end

Sources: backend/perception/temporal/inference.py#18-31 backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#18-31 backend/perception/structural/inference.py#19-24

docs.title: 3.1. Temporal Encoder: Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT)
docs.text:
The Temporal Encoder processes sequential price data to identity patterns, trends, and volatility regimes. It utilizes a Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) architecture, which excels at multi-horizon forecasting and identifying long-term dependencies in time-series data.

  • Model: TemporalFusionTransformer backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py
  • Input: A window of OHLCV (Open, High, Low, Close, Volume) bars defined by OHLCV_WINDOW_MINUTES backend/perception/temporal/inference.py#26
  • Output: A 128-dimensional "price" embedding.
  • Service: The TFTInferenceService subscribes to live ticks via Redis backend/perception/temporal/inference.py#39 and updates embeddings in real-time.

For details, see Temporal Encoder: Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT)

docs.title: 3.2. Semantic Encoder: Distilled Financial LLM
docs.text:
The Semantic Encoder translates unstructured textual data —such as news headlines and article bodies— into numerical sentiment vectors. To maintain low latency, the system uses a 15M-parameter student model distilled from a larger FinBERT teacher.

  • Model: DistilledFinancialEncoder backend/perception/semantic/distilled_llm.py
  • Input: Tokenized text from channel:news.global backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#39
  • Output: A 64-dimensional "semantic" embedding.
  • Service: SemanticInferenceService handles incoming news events, tokenizes them using the AutoTokenizer, and generates embeddings for all mentioned tickers backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#65-81

For details, see Semantic Encoder: Distilled Financial LLM.

docs.title: 3.3. Structural Encoder: Graph Attention Network (GATv2)
docs.text:
The Structural Encoder captures the interconnected nature of the market, modeling dependencies such as supply-chain relationships and sector correlations. It uses a Graph Attention Network (GATv2) to propagate information across a global market graph.

  • Model: GraphEncoder backend/perception/structural/gat_model.py
  • Input: A graph object Data containing node features (price momentum) and edge_index (supply-chain links) backend/perception/structural/inference.py#26-31
  • Service: GraphInferenceService runs on a daily cadence, rebuilding the global graph via build_graph_data and updating the structural context for the entire universe backend/perception/structural/inference.py#99-121

For details, see Structural Encoder: Graph Attention Network (GATv2)

docs.title: Semantic Encoder: Distilled Financial LLM
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/perception/semantic/inference.py
  • docker/Dockerfile.api
  • pyproject.toml

The Semantic Encoder is the natural language processing component of the Lumina V3 'Chimera' architecture. It transforms unstructured financial text (news headlines, article bodies, and social sentiment) into a compact 64-dimensional semantic embedding space. To meet the system's sub-second latency requirements while maintaining the reasoning capabilities of large-scale financial models, the system employs a knowledge distillation a knowledge distillation pipeline where a large FinBERT teacher trains a high-performance 15M-parameter studentmodel.

docs.title: Distillation Pipeline and Model Architecture
docs.text: The core of the semantic subsystem is the DistilledFinancialEncoder. While the teacher model (FinBERT) contains approximately 110M parameters, the student is optimized for inference speed and memory efficiency, fitting in less than 50 MB at fp16 precision pyproject.toml#111-113.

docs.title: The DistilledFinancialEncoder
docs.text:
The model architecture consists of a lightweight backbone followed by a projection head that compresses the hidden state into the final 64-d embedding.

Component Specification Teacher Model ProsusAI/finbert Student Parameters ~15 Million Input Sequence 256 tokens (max_length) Output Latent 64-dimensional semantic vector

docs.title: Model Logic Flow
docs.text: The DistilledFinancialEncoder produces two outputs: the semantic embedding used for trading decisions and a classification logit (typically sentiment or relevance) used during the distillation phase to align student behavior with the teacher.

docs.title: Semantic Encoder Internal Data Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Natural Language Space"
        NewsEvent["News Event (JSON)"] --> HBS["Headline + Body<br/>String"]
    end
    subgraph DistilledFinancialEncoder
        HBS --> Tokenizer["Tokenizer<br/>(AutoTokenizer)"]
        Tokenizer --> TB["Transformer<br/>Backbone (15M<br/>Params)"]
        TB --> PL["Pooler Layer"] --> PH["Projection Head"]
        PH --> Embedding["64-d Embedding"]
        PL --> CH["Classification Head"] --> SL["Sentiment Logits"]
    end
    subgraph "Code Entity Space"
        Embedding --> EmbeddingWriter --> RedisCache
    end

Sources: backend/perception/semantic/distilled_llm.py#10-15 backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#56-69 backend/perception/common/embedding_writer.py#1-10

docs.title: Semantic Inference Service
docs.text: The SemanticInferenceService handles the real-time processing of news streams. It operates as an asynchronous consumer, subscribing to global news channels and updating the Feature Store (Redis) with fresh embeddings.

docs.title: Key Implementation Details
docs.text:
• Subscription: The service listens to the channel:news.global Redis pub/sub channel backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#38-39.
  • Handling Events: Upon receiving a message, it concatenates the headline and body, tokenizes the text, and runs a forward pass through the distilled model backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#65-81
  • Ticker Mapping: A single news can affect multiple assets. The service iterates through the tickers list in the vent metadata and writes the same semantic vector to each ticker's key in Redis via the EmbeddingWriter backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#80-81.

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Redis as Redis (channel:news.global)
    participant SemanticInferenceService
    participant DistilledFinancialEncoder
    participant EmbeddingWriter

    Redis ->> SemanticInferenceService: News Event JSON
    SemanticInferenceService ->> SemanticInferenceService: _handle(event)
    SemanticInferenceService ->> DistilledFinancialEncoder: Forward Pass (input_ids | mask)
    DistilledFinancialEncoder -->> SemanticInferenceService: 64-d Vector

    loop for each ticker in even
        SemanticInferenceService ->> EmbeddingWriter: write("semantic" | ticker | vector)
        EmbeddingWriter ->> Redis: SET semantic:TICKER <vector>
    end

Sources: backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#34-71 backend/perception/common/embedding_writer.py#15-25

docs.title: XAI: Integrated Gradients Attribution
docs.text:
For the Lumina V3 system to be "explainable" (XAI), it must identify which specific words in a news article triggered a trading decision. This is handled by the Captum library integration pyproject.toml#104-108.

When the Spartan Arena identifies a "divergence point" (where the agent's action differs significantly from expected behavior), it can trigger the Integrated-Gradients path. This calculates the attribution of the output embedding back to the input tokens, allowing the RunSummarizer to generate narratives like: "Agent sold due to high attribution on tokens ['banckruptcy', 'litigation', 'default']" pyproject.toml#155.

docs.title: Deployment and Dependencies
docs.text:
The semantic encoder is part of the perception service layer. It is designed to run on either CPU or GPU, though GPU is prefered for the teacher-student training phase.

  • Library Requirements: Depends on torch, transformers, and captum pyproject.toml#79-80 pyproject.toml#107.
  • Quantization: While the student is small, the gpu extra (including bitsandbytes) is available to run the 110M-parameter teacher on memory-constrained hardware during distillation pyproject.toml#110-117.
  • Checkpointing: The service looks for trained weights at models/semantic/best.pt. If missing, it falls back to random weights with a warning, requiring a run of the trainer script first backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#74-104.

Sources: pyproject.toml#73-88 backend/perception/semantic/inference.py#74-104

docs.title: Structural Encoder: Graph Attention Network (GATv2)
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/perception/structural/inference.py
  • notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb

The Structural Encoder is the third modality-specific encoder in the Perception Layer. It captures inter-asset dependencies and systemic risk propagation by modelling the market as a heterogeneous graph. Unlike the Temporal (TFT) and Semantic (LLM) encoders which process assets in isolation, the GATv2 encoder produces a 32-dimensional structural pressure embedding that reflects an asset's position within the broader supply-chain and correlation network.

docs.title: Graph Architecture and Data Flow
docs.text: The structural pipeline operates on a daily cadence, constructing a graph where nodes represent tickers and edges represent either static supply-chain relationships or dynamic price correlations.

docs.title: System-to-Code Entity Mapping
docs.text: The following diagram bridges the high-level structural concepts to the specific code implementation responsible for graph construction and inference.

docs.title: Structural Encoding Pipeline
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Data Engine Space"
        direction TB
        TimescaleStore[(TimescaleStore)]
        RedisCache[(RedisCache)]
    end
    subgraph "Graph Construction"
        BGD["build_graph_data()"]
        SCE["supply_chain_edges<br/>(DB Table)"]
        OHLCV["ohlcv_1m (DB Table)"]
    end
    subgraph "Inference Service"
        GraphInferenceService
        GraphEncoder["GraphEncoder (GATv2)"]
        EmbeddingWriter
    end
    TimescaleStore --> |"fetch edges"| BGD
    TimescaleStore --> |"fetch daily returns"| BGD
    BGD --> |"torch_geometric.Data"| GraphInferenceService
    GraphInferenceService --> |"forward pass"| GraphEncoder
    GraphEncoder --> |"32-d latent z"| GraphInferenceService
    GraphInferenceService --> |"write('graph',<br/>ticker, vec)"| EmbeddingWriter
    EmbeddingWriter --> |"SET graph:TICKER"| RedisCache

Sources: backend/perception/structural/inference.py#18-38 backend/perception/structural/inference.py#93-95 backend/perception/structural/graph_builder.py#192

docs.title: Graph Construction (graph_builder)
docs.text:
The graph is built using torch_geometric.data.Data objects notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb It incorporates two distinct edge types to capture different forms of structural "pressure":

  1. Static Edges (Supply Chain): Derived from the supply_chain_edges table. These represent fundamenta dependencies (e.g., NVDA supplying AAPL) notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb
  2. Dynamic Edges (Correlation): Generated based on Pearson correlation of log returns over a lookback period. Edges are created when \(|r|>0.6\) notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb

docs.title: Node Feature Composition
docs.text:
Each node (ticker) is initialized with a 32-dimensional feature vector x before being processed by the GATv2 layers:

  • Sector One-Hot: (NUM_SECTORS dims) Identifies the asset class notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb
  • Log Market Cap: (1 dim) Scaled representation of asset size notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb
  • Realized Volatility: (1 dim) 252-day annualized volatility notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb
  • Beta vs SPY: (1 dim) Systematic risk exposure notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb
  • Padding: Reserved zeros to maintain the 32-dim structural interface notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb

Sources: notebooks/04_graph_construction.ipynb

docs.title: GATv2 Model Architecture (GraphEncoder)
docs.text: The GraphEncoder utilizes the GATv2 (Graph Attention Network v2) operator, which improves upon standard GAT by implementing "dynamic attention", allowing every node to attend to every other node with a more flexible ranking mechanism.

docs.title: GATv2 Information Flow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Input Layer"
        NF["Node Features (32-d)"]
        EI["Edge Index"]
        EA["Edge Attr"]
    end
    subgraph "GATv2 Stack"
        GATC1["GATv2Conv (Head 1)"]
        ELU["ELU Activation"]
        GATC2["GATv2Conv (Head 2)"]
    end
    subgraph "Output"
        SE["Structural<br/>Embedding (32-d)"]
    end
    NF --> GATC1
    EI --> GATC1
    EA --> GATC1
    GATC1 --> ELU --> GATC2 --> SE

Sources: backend/perception/structural/gat_model.py#15 backend/perception/structural/inference.py#31

The model produces a latent representation \(z \in \mathbb{R}^{32}\) for every node in the graph. This embedding encapsulates not just the asset's own state, but the state of its neighbors weighted by their attention scores (structural pressure).

docs.title: Inference and Persistence
docs.text: The GraphInferenceService runs as a background daemon, typically on a daily interval (_DAILY_INTERVAL_S = 86400) backend/perception/structural/inference.py#43

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text:
• run_once(data, tickers_order): Performs the forward pass on the GPU and converts the resulting tensor to a NumPy array backend/perception/structural/inference.py#26-39
  • EmbeddingWriter.write(): Commits the 32-d vectors to Redis using the key format graph:{ticker} backend/perception/structural/inference.py#36-37
  • _pick_device(): Handles fallback logic, attempting to use CUDA but reverting to CPU if the probe fails backend/perception/structural/inference.py#45-53

docs.title: Data Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. Trigger: The service wakes up every 24 hours backend/perception/structural/inference.py#99
  2. Fetch: build_graph_data queries TimescaleDB for the latest prices and edges backend/perception/structural/inference.py#101
  3. Compute: The GraphEncoder processes the full market graph in a single batch backend/perception/structural/inference.py#32
  4. Store: Embeddings are written to Redis, where the StateAssembler will later retrieves them for fusion backend/perception/structural/inference.py#37

Sources: backend/perception/structural/inference.py#18-38 backend/perception/structural/inference.py#82-102

docs.title: Temporal Encoder: Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT)
docs.text:
Relevant source files
  • backend/cognition/training/behavioral_cloning.py
  • backend/config/constants.py
  • backend/perception/temporal/inference.py
  • notebooks/02_tft_prototype.ipynb

The Temporal Encoder is the first component of the Perception Layer in the Chimera architecture. It is responsible for transforming raw multi-variate price data (OHLCV) into a high-dimensional Temporal Embedding (\(d=128\)). It utilizes a custom implementation of the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) (Lim et al., 2021), optimized for financial time-series by incorporating gated residual networks and variable selection to handle noisy market data.

docs.title: 1. Architecture Overview
docs.text: The TemporalFusionTransformer class backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py#60-72 implements a multi-stage pipeline designed to extract both long-term dependencies and local patterns from market bars.

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text:
• Gated Residual Network (GRN): The fundamental building block backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py#28-32 It provides adaptive non-linear processing with a GLU (Gated Linear Unit) to skip unnecessary steps, preventing vanishing gradients in deep financial networks.
  • Variable Selection Network (VSN): Performs instance-specific feature selection backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py#42-57 It assign weights to different OHLCV inputs, allowing the model to ignore features (e.g., Volume) if they are currently non-informative.
  • LSTM Encoder: Processes the sequence of variable-selected features to capture local temporal context backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py#85-91
  • Interpretable Multi-Head Attention: A specialized attention mechanism that allows the model to focus on specific past events (e.g., a flash crash 30 minutes ago) backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py#93-98
  • Temporal Embedding Output: The final state is projected to a 128-dimensional vector defined by DIM_PRICE backend/config/constants.py#56-57

docs.title: Data Flow Diagram: TFT Internal Logic
docs.text:
This diagram maps the logical flow of data through the PyTorch entities defined in tft_model.py.

flowchart TB
    subgraph "Input Processing"
        Raw["Raw OHLCV Tensor (Batch,<br/>Window,5)"]
        VariableSelectionNetwork
        GRN["GRN (Input Transformation)"]
        Raw --> VariableSelectionNetwork --> GRN
    end

    subgraph "Temporal Processing"
        GRN --> LSTM["LSTM (Sequence Encoder)"]
        LSTM --> InterpretableMultiHeadAttention
    end

    subgraph "Output Generation"
        InterpretableMultiHeadAttention --> PAGRN["Post-Attention GRN"]
        PAGRN --> GAN["GateAddNorm (Residual<br/>Connection)"]
        GAN --> LP["Linear Projection (128-d)"]
        LP --> TE["Temporal Embedding<br/>(DIM_PRICE)"]
    end

Sources: backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py#25-137 backend/config/constants.py#56-57

docs.title: Variable Selection Network (VSN)
docs.text:
The VSN is critical for financial data where the signal-to-noise ratio varies. It computes a vector of weights \(\alpha\) for the input features and produces a weighted sum of transformed features.

  • Function: VariableSelectionNetwork.forward backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py#50-57
  • Mechanism: It uses a GRN to produce weights for each input variable and another set of GRNs to transform each individual variable before weighting them.

docs.title: Gated Residual Network (GRN)
docs.text:
The GRN allows the model to adaptively control the flow of information.

  • Function: GatedResidualNetwork.forward backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py#35-39
  • Logic: x -> Linear -> ELU -> Linear -> GLU -> AddNorm.

docs.title: Dimensional Constants
docs.text:
The TFT's output is strictly governed by project-wide constants to ensure compatibility with the DeepFusionNexus.

Constant Value Description DIM_PRICE 128 Size of the temporal embedding vector. OHLCV_WINDOW_MINUTES 240 Look-back window (4 hours of 1-min bars). OHLCV_HORIZON_MINUTES 60 Target horizon for self-supervised training.

Sources: backend/config/constants.py#56-95 backend/perception/temporal/tft_model.py#28-140

docs.title: Self-Supervised Pre-training
docs.text: The TFT is pre-trained using a self-supervised task: predicting the forward return over OHLCV_HORIZON_MINUTES backend/config/constants.py#95 This ensures the encoder learns to extract features relevant to price movement before being integrated into the RL agent's policy.

docs.title: Preprocessing Pipeline
docs.text:
1. Normalization: The TFTInferenceService applies Min-Max scaling to the input buffer backend/perception/temporal/inference.py#68
  2. Windowing: A sliding window of 240 bars is maintained in a deque backend/perception/temporal/inference.py#33
  3. Outlier Handling: The prototype utilizes RobustScaler to mitigate the impact of price spikes notebooks/02_tft_prototype.ipynb

Sources: backend/perception/temporal/inference.py#31-62 notebooks/02_tft_prototype.ipynb

docs.title: 4. Inference Service
docs.text: The TFTInferenceService handles real-time embedding generation. It subscribes to a Redis tick stream and writes embeddings back to the Feature Store.

docs.title: System Integration Diagram
docs.text:
This diagram shows how the TFTInferenceService interacts with the broader system components.

flowchart LR
    RedisCache[("RedisCache (Tick<br/>Stream)")]

    subgraph "Inference Loop"
        TFTInferenceService
        TFTInferenceService --> |Update| Deque["`_buffers (deque)`"]
        Deque --> |"Window: 240"| TemporalFusionTransformer --> |"forward()"| Embedding["128-d Embedding"]
    end

    RedisCache --> |"`subscribe_ticks()`"| TFTInferenceService
    Embedding --> |"`write('price', ...)`"| EmbeddingWriter --> Redis[("Redis (Feature<br/>Store)")]

docs.title: Key Functions

docs.title: 5. Verification: Prototype Over-fitting
docs.text:
The architectural integrity of the TFT was verified in notebooks/02_tft_prototype.ipynb.

  • Goal: Over-fit the model on 200 samples of SPY 1h bars notebooks/02_tft_prototype.ipynb
  • Criterion: Achieve a Training MSE < 1e-3 within 60 epochs notebooks/02_tft_prototype.ipynb
  • Observation: Successful over-fitting confirms that gradients flow correctly through the complex GRN/VSN/Attention stack notebooks/02_tft_prototype.ipynb

Sources: notebooks/02_tft_prototype.ipynb
